var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get the stored results from the query variables
books_file = var_functions.query_db:33
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db:30

# Load data from files
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract publication years
def extract_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    patterns = [
        r'(?:published|released)[:\s]*(?:on|in|January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)?[:\s,]*\D*(\d{4})',
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[\s,]+\d{1,2},?\s*(\d{4})'
    ]
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    return None

# Process books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        book['publication_year'] = year
        book['decade'] = f"{year//10*10}s"
        # Extract numeric ID
        match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        book['numeric_id'] = match.group(1) if match else None
        books_with_years.append(book)

# Process reviews
for review in reviews:
    match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    review['numeric_id'] = match.group(1) if match else None
    review['rating'] = float(review['rating'])

# Join on numeric IDs
rating_by_book = {}
for review in reviews:
    if review['numeric_id']:
        rating_by_book[review['numeric_id']] = review['rating']

# Aggregate by decade
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for book in books_with_years:
    if book['numeric_id'] and book['numeric_id'] in rating_by_book:
        decade = book['decade']
        rating = rating_by_book[book['numeric_id']]
        decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
        decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book['numeric_id'])

# Calculate averages for decades with at least 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    if len(stats['books']) >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': len(stats['books']),
            'rating_count': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by avg rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'top_decade': results[0] if results else None,
    'all_decades': results[:10],
    'total_decades': len(results)
}, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get the stored results from the query variables
books_file = var_functions.query_db:33
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db:30

# Load data from files
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract publication years
def extract_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    patterns = [
        r'(?:published|released)[:\s]*(?:on|in|January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)?[:\s,]*\D*(\d{4})',
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[\s,]+\d{1,2},?\s*(\d{4})'
    ]
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    return None

# Process books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    details = book.get('details', '')
    year = extract_year(details)
    if year:
        book['publication_year'] = year
        book['decade'] = f"{year//10*10}s"
        # Extract numeric ID
        match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        book['numeric_id'] = match.group(1) if match else None
        books_with_years.append(book)

# Process reviews
for review in reviews:
    match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    review['numeric_id'] = match.group(1) if match else None
    review['rating'] = float(review['rating'])

# Join on numeric IDs
rating_by_book = {}
for review in reviews:
    if review['numeric_id']:
        rating_by_book[review['numeric_id']] = review['rating']

# Aggregate by decade
decade_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for book in books_with_years:
    if book['numeric_id'] and book['numeric_id'] in rating_by_book:
        decade = book['decade']
        rating = rating_by_book[book['numeric_id']]
        decade_stats[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
        decade_stats[decade]['books'].add(book['numeric_id'])

# Calculate averages for decades with at least 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade, stats in decade_stats.items():
    if len(stats['books']) >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(stats['ratings']) / len(stats['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': avg_rating,
            'book_count': len(stats['books']),
            'rating_count': len(stats['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by avg rating descending
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'top_decade': results[0] if results else None,
    'all_decades': results[:10],
    'total_decades': len(results)
}, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

She specializes in showing teachers how kids' invented spellings and miscues can help us work with them in more sophisticated and learner-centered ways. var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# The file paths are stored in variables with these names
books_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:33')
reviews_file = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:30')

# Load books data from file
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data from file
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded', len(books), 'books and', len(reviews), 'reviews')

# Show samples
print('Sample book:', books[0])
print('Sample review:', reviews[0])
print('__RESULT__: Data loaded successfully')

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

result.preview: "Execution failed with exit code 1\nFileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/tmp/tmp2v5_2y5h.json'"

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get file paths for the query results
books_file = '/tmp/tmp2v5_2y5h.json'
reviews_file = '/tmp/tmprv2g1f4p.json'

# Load the data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract year from book details
def extract_year_from_details(details_str):
    if not details_str:
        return None
    # Look for year patterns like "2004", "1996", etc.
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', details_str)
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        # Validate reasonable book publication years
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    return None

# Process books - extract year and numeric ID
books_processed = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year_from_details(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        # Extract numeric ID from book_id (e.g., "bookid_1" -> "1")
        book_id_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        if book_id_match:
            numeric_id = book_id_match.group(1)
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            books_processed.append({
                'numeric_id': numeric_id,
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade,
                'book_id': book['book_id']
            })

# Process reviews - extract numeric ID and rating
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    # Extract numeric ID from purchase_id (e.g., "purchaseid_186" -> "186")
    purchase_id_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if purchase_id_match:
        numeric_id = purchase_id_match.group(1)
        reviews_processed.append({
            'numeric_id': numeric_id,
            'rating': float(review['rating']),
            'purchase_id': review['purchase_id']
        })

# Create rating lookup by numeric ID
rating_lookup = {}
for review in reviews_processed:
    rating_lookup[review['numeric_id']] = review['rating']

# Match books with ratings and aggregate by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for book in books_processed:
    if book['numeric_id'] in rating_lookup:
        decade = book['decade']
        rating = rating_lookup[book['numeric_id']]
        decade_books[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
        decade_books[decade]['books'].add(book['numeric_id'])

# Calculate statistics for decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, data in decade_books.items():
    if len(data['books']) >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'book_count': len(data['books']),
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Return the result in the required format
result = {
    'top_decade': decade_stats[0] if decade_stats else None,
    'all_decades': decade_stats,
    'books_processed': len(books_processed),
    'reviews_processed': len(reviews_processed)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Referance Guide
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Good reference guide for the basics

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get file paths for the query results
books_file = '/tmp/tmp2v5_2y5h.json'
reviews_file = '/tmp/tmprv2g1f4p.json'

# Load the data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract year from book details
def extract_year_from_details(details_str):
    if not details_str:
        return None
    # Look for year patterns like "2004", "1996", etc.
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', details_str)
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        # Validate reasonable book publication years
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    return None

# Process books - extract year and numeric ID
books_processed = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year_from_details(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        # Extract numeric ID from book_id (e.g., "bookid_1" -> "1")
        book_id_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        if book_id_match:
            numeric_id = book_id_match.group(1)
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            books_processed.append({
                'numeric_id': numeric_id,
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade,
                'book_id': book['book_id']
            })

# Process reviews - extract numeric ID and rating
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    # Extract numeric ID from purchase_id (e.g., "purchaseid_186" -> "186")
    purchase_id_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if purchase_id_match:
        numeric_id = purchase_id_match.group(1)
        reviews_processed.append({
            'numeric_id': numeric_id,
            'rating': float(review['rating']),
            'purchase_id': review['purchase_id']
        })

# Create rating lookup by numeric ID
rating_lookup = {}
for review in reviews_processed:
    rating_lookup[review['numeric_id']] = review['rating']

# Match books with ratings and aggregate by decade
decade_books = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for book in books_processed:
    if book['numeric_id'] in rating_lookup:
        decade = book['decade']
        rating = rating_lookup[book['numeric_id']]
        decade_books[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)
        decade_books[decade]['books'].add(book['numeric_id'])

# Calculate statistics for decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, data in decade_books.items():
    if len(data['books']) >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'book_count': len(data['books']),
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Return the result in the required format
result = {
    'top_decade': decade_stats[0] if decade_stats else None,
    'all_decades': decade_stats,
    'books_processed': len(books_processed),
    'reviews_processed': len(reviews_processed)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:2.description: ["About the Author", "SANDRA WILDE, Ph.D., is widely recognized for her expertise in developmental spelling and her advocacy of holistic approaches to spelling and phonics. She is Professor of Curriculum and Instruction at Portland State University in Oregon. She is best known for her work in invented spelling, phonics and miscue analysis. She specializes in showing teachers how kids' invented spellings and miscues can help us work with them in more sophisticated and learner-centered ways. Looking at what kids do as they read and write is at the heart of Sandra's presentations and workshops. She can do lively keynote presentations that highlight the interesting things that we can learn by paying close attention to students' invented spellings and miscues, as well as workshops of varying lengths that focus on student-centered teaching of spelling and phonics. She has recently begun offering workshops that focus on understanding students' miscues as a guide to appropriate instruction, p"]

var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

She is Professor of Curriculum and Instruction at Portland State University in Oregon. She is best known for her work in invented spelling, phonics and miscue analysis. She specializes in showing teachers how kids' invented spellings and miscues can help us work with them in more sophisticated and learner-centered ways. Looking at what kids do as they read and write is at the heart of Sandra's presentations and workshops. She can do lively keynote presentations that highlight the interesting things that we can learn by paying close attention to students' invented spellings and miscues, as well as workshops of varying lengths that focus on student-centered teaching of spelling and phonics. She has recently begun offering workshops that focus on understanding students' miscues as a guide to appropriate instruction, p"]

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# List all variables starting with 'var_'
var_names = [var for var in locals() if var.startswith('var_')]
print('Found variables:', var_names)

# Access the most recent query results
books_result = locals()['var_functions.query_db:33']
reviews_result = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']

print('Books result type:', type(books_result))
print('Reviews result type:', type(reviews_result))

# Load the data based on type
if isinstance(books_result, str) and '.json' in books_result:
    print('Loading books from file:', books_result)
    with open(books_result, 'r') as f:
        books = json.load(f)
else:
    books = books_result
    
if isinstance(reviews_result, str) and '.json' in reviews_result:
    print('Loading reviews from file:', reviews_result)
    with open(reviews_result, 'r') as f:
        reviews = json.load(f)
else:
    reviews = reviews_result

print(f'Loaded {len(books)} books and {len(reviews)} reviews')

# Process books to extract publication years
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    # Look for 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    matches = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details_text)
    for match in matches:
        year = int(match)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    return None

# Extract publication years and numeric IDs
books_processed = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        # Extract numeric ID from book_id
        match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            books_processed.append({
                'numeric_id': numeric_id,
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade,
                'book_id': book['book_id']
            })

# Process reviews
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    # Extract numeric ID
    match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if match:
        numeric_id = match.group(1)
        reviews_processed.append({
            'numeric_id': numeric_id,
            'rating': float(review['rating']),
            'purchase_id': review['purchase_id']
        })

print(f'Processed {len(books_processed)} books with years and {len(reviews_processed)} reviews')

# Match reviews to books and aggregate by decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for review in reviews_processed:
    for book in books_processed:
        if book['numeric_id'] == review['numeric_id']:
            decade = book['decade']
            decade_ratings[decade]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
            decade_ratings[decade]['books'].add(book['numeric_id'])
            break

print(f'Found data for {len(decade_ratings)} decades')

# Calculate average ratings for decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, data in decade_ratings.items():
    book_count = len(data['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'book_count': book_count,
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Print results in required format
result = {
    'decade_stats': decade_stats,
    'top_decade': decade_stats[0] if decade_stats else None
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# List all variables starting with 'var_'
var_names = [var for var in locals() if var.startswith('var_')]
print('Found variables:', var_names)

# Access the most recent query results
books_result = locals()['var_functions.query_db:33']
reviews_result = locals()['var_functions.query_db:30']

print('Books result type:', type(books_result))
print('Reviews result type:', type(reviews_result))

# Load the data based on type
if isinstance(books_result, str) and '.json' in books_result:
    print('Loading books from file:', books_result)
    with open(books_result, 'r') as f:
        books = json.load(f)
else:
    books = books_result
    
if isinstance(reviews_result, str) and '.json' in reviews_result:
    print('Loading reviews from file:', reviews_result)
    with open(reviews_result, 'r') as f:
        reviews = json.load(f)
else:
    reviews = reviews_result

print(f'Loaded {len(books)} books and {len(reviews)} reviews')

# Process books to extract publication years
def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    # Look for 4-digit years between 1900-2023
    matches = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details_text)
    for match in matches:
        year = int(match)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    return None

# Extract publication years and numeric IDs
books_processed = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        # Extract numeric ID from book_id
        match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
        if match:
            numeric_id = match.group(1)
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            books_processed.append({
                'numeric_id': numeric_id,
                'year': year,
                'decade': decade,
                'book_id': book['book_id']
            })

# Process reviews
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    # Extract numeric ID
    match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if match:
        numeric_id = match.group(1)
        reviews_processed.append({
            'numeric_id': numeric_id,
            'rating': float(review['rating']),
            'purchase_id': review['purchase_id']
        })

print(f'Processed {len(books_processed)} books with years and {len(reviews_processed)} reviews')

# Match reviews to books and aggregate by decade
decade_ratings = defaultdict(lambda: {'ratings': [], 'books': set()})

for review in reviews_processed:
    for book in books_processed:
        if book['numeric_id'] == review['numeric_id']:
            decade = book['decade']
            decade_ratings[decade]['ratings'].append(review['rating'])
            decade_ratings[decade]['books'].add(book['numeric_id'])
            break

print(f'Found data for {len(decade_ratings)} decades')

# Calculate average ratings for decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = []
for decade, data in decade_ratings.items():
    book_count = len(data['books'])
    if book_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        decade_stats.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'book_count': book_count,
            'rating_count': len(data['ratings'])
        })

# Sort by average rating
decade_stats.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

# Print results in required format
result = {
    'decade_stats': decade_stats,
    'top_decade': decade_stats[0] if decade_stats else None
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

She can do lively keynote presentations that highlight the interesting things that we can learn by paying close attention to students' invented spellings and miscues, as well as workshops of varying lengths that focus on student-centered teaching of spelling and phonics. She has recently begun offering workshops that focus on understanding students' miscues as a guide to appropriate instruction, p"]

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!
